name: Alto de la Mina, Humaran and Puerto de Las Muñecas — circular from Balmaseda
name_original: Alto de la Mina, Humaran y Puerto de Las Muñecas — circular desde Balmaseda
description: This difficult 56.4-mile road cycling route from Balmaseda climbs Alto de la Mina, Humaran, and Puerto de Las Muñecas, offering scenic views
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Balmaseda traverse diverse natural landscapes, characterized by mountains, forests, and rivers. The region features varying elevations, from river valleys to prominent peaks like Mount Kolitza, offering a range of challenges for cyclists. The terrain includes a mix of paved and quiet roads, with some unpaved sections, providing varied experiences for road cyclists. Best road cycling routes around Balmaseda The most popular road cycling route is Alto de la Mina, Humaran and Puerto de Las Muñecas — circular from Balmaseda, a 56.4 miles (90.8 km) trail that takes 4 hours 50 minutes to complete. This difficult route features significant elevation gains across multiple mountain passes. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Old Bridge of Balmaseda – Oxirando Tower loop from La Calzada, a moderate 37.1 miles (59.7 km) path. This route includes views of the historic Old Bridge of Balmaseda and offers varied terrain. Local road cyclists also love the Oxirando Tower – Sodupe loop from La Herrera, a 20.2 miles (32.6 km) trail leading through varied landscapes with moderate elevation, often completed in about 1 hour 21 minutes. Road cycling around Balmaseda is defined by mountainous terrain, river valleys, and forested areas, with routes offering options for different ability levels. The routes in Balmaseda are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.7 stars from over 40 reviews. More than 2,700 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Balmaseda's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What types of road cycling routes can I find around Balmaseda?
A: Balmaseda offers a diverse range of road cycling routes, characterized by mountainous terrain, river valleys, and forested areas. You'll find routes with varying elevations, from moderate climbs to significant ascents up peaks like Mount Kolitza. The paths include a mix of paved and quiet roads, catering to different skill levels and preferences.

Q: How many road cycling routes are available in the Balmaseda area?
A: There are over 50 road cycling routes available around Balmaseda. These routes are categorized by difficulty, with 12 easy, 26 moderate, and 15 difficult options, ensuring there's something for every cyclist.

Q: Are there any easy road cycling routes suitable for beginners or casual rides?
A: Yes, Balmaseda has 12 easy road cycling routes. An example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/39565076" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Oxirando Tower – Sodupe loop from La Herrera&lt;/a&gt;, which is 20.2 miles (32.6 km) long with moderate elevation, typically completed in about 1 hour 21 minutes.

Q: What are some challenging road cycling routes for experienced cyclists?
A: For experienced cyclists seeking a challenge, Balmaseda offers 15 difficult routes. A notable option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1032191255/alto-de-la-mina-humaran-and-puerto-de-las-munecas-circular-from-balmaseda" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Alto de la Mina, Humaran and Puerto de Las Muñecas — circular from Balmaseda&lt;/a&gt;. This 56.4-mile (90.8 km) route features significant elevation gains across multiple mountain passes.

Q: What kind of landmarks and viewpoints can I expect to see along the routes?
A: Road cycling routes around Balmaseda often pass by historical and natural landmarks. You can encounter sites like the majestic 13th-century &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/154436" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Old Bridge of Balmaseda&lt;/a&gt;, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/154440" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Church of San Severino&lt;/a&gt;, or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/154441" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;San Roque Hermitage on Mount Kolitza&lt;/a&gt;, which offers stunning panoramic views. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5460326" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Oxirando Tower&lt;/a&gt; is another historical point of interest.

Q: Are there circular road cycling routes available?
A: Yes, many road cycling routes around Balmaseda are circular, allowing you to start and end in the same location. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20528001" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Old Bridge of Balmaseda – Oxirando Tower loop from La Calzada&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate 37.1-mile (59.7 km) circular path that includes views of the historic Old Bridge.

Q: What is the best time of year to go road cycling in Balmaseda?
A: Balmaseda's diverse landscapes make it suitable for road cycling across multiple seasons. The region experiences varying weather, so checking local forecasts is always recommended. Spring and autumn generally offer pleasant temperatures and vibrant scenery, while summer can be warm, and winter might present cooler conditions, especially in higher elevations.

Q: What do other cyclists say about road cycling in Balmaseda?
A: The road cycling routes in Balmaseda are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.7 stars from over 40 reviews. Cyclists often praise the varied terrain, the mix of challenging climbs and scenic descents, and the opportunity to explore both natural beauty and historical sites.

Q: Are there any routes that combine historical sights with cycling?
A: Absolutely. Routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31943505" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Old Bridge of Balmaseda – La Herbosa Viewpoint loop from La Calzada&lt;/a&gt; offer a blend of cycling and historical exploration. This route allows you to experience the region's natural beauty while passing by significant landmarks such as the Old Bridge.

Q: What are the typical distances and elevation gains for road cycling routes in Balmaseda?
A: Route distances vary significantly, from shorter rides around 20 miles (32 km) to longer, more demanding routes exceeding 50 miles (80 km). Elevation gains also range widely, with easy routes having minimal climbs and difficult routes featuring significant ascents of over 1,700 meters (5,500 ft) across multiple mountain passes.

Q: Is Balmaseda a good destination for cyclists looking for scenic views?
A: Yes, Balmaseda is an excellent destination for scenic road cycling. The region is nestled amidst mountains, forests, and rivers, offering picturesque views throughout. Routes often incorporate climbs to viewpoints like Mount Kolitza, providing panoramic vistas of the valley and surrounding natural areas.

faqs.question: How difficult is this cycling route?
faqs.answer: This circular route from Balmaseda is categorized as difficult and demands a very good level of fitness. While the surfaces are generally well-paved, it covers a significant distance of approximately 90.8 km with an elevation gain of about 1728 meters, featuring several challenging climbs.

faqs.question: What are the main climbs on the route?
faqs.answer: The route includes several notable climbs. You'll tackle the Alto de la Maya (possibly also known as Alto de la Mina), which is 2.3 km long with an average gradient of 6.39%. Another significant ascent is the Puerto de Las Muñecas, a 6.22 km climb with an average gradient of 5.05%. The Alto de Humaran is also a key feature, known for its sustained length connecting the valleys of Sopuerta and Cadagua.

faqs.question: What scenic highlights can I expect along the way?
faqs.answer: The route offers diverse scenery and points of interest. You'll pass by the historic Church of San Julián de Santullán, which dates back to the early 16th century. There's also a fantastic View of Castro Urdiales from a high point on the national road, offering vistas of the bay. The mountain passes themselves, like the Alto de la Mina Road, are known for their scenic curves.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The best time to cycle this route is generally during the spring and autumn months when the weather is milder and more stable. Summer can also be pleasant, but be prepared for potentially warmer temperatures, especially on climbs. Always check the local weather forecast before you go.

faqs.question: Where can I park when starting this cycling route from Balmaseda?
faqs.answer: Balmaseda, as the starting and ending point, offers various parking options within the town. Specific parking areas for cyclists are usually available near the town center or main access roads. It's advisable to check local signage upon arrival for the most convenient and appropriate spots.

faqs.question: Is public transport available to the starting point in Balmaseda?
faqs.answer: Yes, Balmaseda is accessible by public transport. Depending on your starting location, you can reach Balmaseda via bus services, which often have connections to larger nearby cities like Bilbao. Check local transport schedules for the most up-to-date information on routes and timings.

faqs.question: Do I need any permits or pay entrance fees for this route?
faqs.answer: No, you do not need any special permits or have to pay entrance fees to cycle this route. It primarily uses public roads and mountain passes in the Basque Country, Spain, which are freely accessible.

faqs.question: Is this road cycling route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: As this is a road cycling route primarily on public roads, it is generally not considered suitable or safe for cycling with dogs. The high speeds, traffic, and significant climbs make it challenging for pets to accompany you.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this cycling route?
faqs.answer: This route is quite long and challenging. On average, it takes approximately 4 hours and 50 minutes of active cycling time to complete, but this can vary significantly based on your fitness level, speed, and any stops you make for breaks or sightseeing.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this cycling route?
faqs.answer: The route primarily features well-paved road surfaces, making it generally smooth for road bikes. Despite its overall 'difficult' rating, the quality of the roads contributes to a good cycling experience. You will encounter significant elevation changes with sustained climbs and descents.

faqs.question: Does the route intersect other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, sections of this route intersect or run alongside other paths. You'll find parts of the Camino Olvidado (Bilbao-Villasante), Zalla-Balmaseda Bidegorria-Vía Verde de Kadagua, and the Northern Route (Cantabria) along the way.
